texts.original:
My topic for this course touches on randomness, and coincidentally (though most probably not coincidence at all), I am also considering randomness as part of another project for another course. I am a great admirer of serendipity. Actually, depending on your world view, you could consider every event in life to be random, and then there are happenings that you consider destiny. Every person you cross in the supermarket is random and negligible, but as soon as it is that certain person and it is not at all their area, it seems like a very random but symbolic experience. Destiny is just the selection of random events you choose to give value to, right? I still remember the quote by Milan Kundera, even though I read that book many years ago: Chance and chance alone has a message for us. Everything that occurs out of necessity, everything expected, repeated day in and day out, is mute. Only chance can speak to us.
Maybe it is just a very romantic view on life to give certain events a deeper meaning, to attach yourself, your day, your memory, your attention to this unforeseeable moment. Sometimes you have to make room in your everyday life to allow these encounters to happen, to let go of control and embrace everything that comes your way with the intention of it being something important. Sometimes it occurs in a moment where you least expect it, like in the supermarket from the example above. Some people do not think about the chain of causal events that may have led to the random situation, while others try to manipulate their and other people’s life to converge into a desired event. Maybe seeing something as random enhances its value? Appreciating the minute probability of it happening as if it meant it could still be taken away from you, as if the low chances also meant little time and presence. Or does it reduce the value of a person or a moment, as stochasticity equals meaninglessness? 
Anyways, just random thoughts on this topic ...
iterations: My coursework orbits around the dance of randomness, and in what may be fate's own brushstroke (or perhaps a deliberately whispered coincidence), I find myself equally immersed in exploring chaos within another creative endeavor. I am enchanted by serendipity's gentle pull. The world, depending on one's gaze, unfolds as an endless tapestry of happenstance, yet certain threads shimmer with the weight of destiny. The anonymous figure glimpsed in fluorescent aisles dissolves into pure chance, yet when that same soul resurfaces in an utterly foreign context, it crystallizes into something luminous and strange. Destiny, perhaps, is simply the constellation of random moments we choose to illuminate with our attention. Kundera's words linger like watercolor bleeding across paper: "Chance and chance alone has a message for us. Everything that occurs out of necessity, everything expected, repeated day in and day out, is mute. Only chance can speak to us." There exists an almost melancholic beauty in inscribing meaning onto fleeting encounters, in surrendering to the poetry of the unexpected. To commune with these whispered moments, one must release the reins of control and become a vessel for whatever arrives unbidden. These crystalline instances materialize when we least anticipate them, fragmenting our ordinary landscapes. Some drift through life unquestioning, while others meticulously choreograph their existence toward specific horizons. Does randomness amplify a moment's luminescence? Perhaps the improbability of something occurring renders it sacred, fragile as spun glass. Yet might chaos also hollow meaning away? These are merely preliminary sketches, impressions upon the canvas of contemplation.

Engagement with stochastic matrices unfolds as a process of recursive deconstruction, wherein the boundaries between calculated intervention and veiled manifestation blur into indeterminacy itself. The framework resists crystallization; serendipity functions less as phenomenon than as a modality of consciousness encountering its own contingency. Reality dissolves into lattices of potentiality—nodes where epistemological positioning generates meaning retroactively rather than prospectively. The stranger becomes a trace, a fluctuation within probability fields that may or may not resolve into coherence upon subsequent emergence across disparate topological spaces. Destiny emerges as a posteriori construction, the selective illumination of arbitrary nodes within infinite networks. The utterance itself—"Chance alone speaks"—reverberates as absence masquerading as message, signification haunting the void of necessity. Encounter becomes displacement; meaning becomes the imposition of pattern upon fundamentally resistant materiality. The act of witnessing constitutes a collapse of superposition, yet this collapse generates only provisional architectures of sense that perpetually threaten dissolution. Luminescence and opacity interpenetrate; the crystalline moment fragments under scrutiny. Improbability oscillates between consecration and evacuation, neither quite present, neither quite absent. The question of whether stochastic processes intensify or diminish significance itself dissolves into a problem of measurement—whether attention creates the phenomenon it purports to observe. What remains are movements toward articulation perpetually interrupted by their own groundlessness, sketches drawn upon surfaces that resist permanence.

One's engagement with aleatory systems unveils the rarefied interpenetration of instrumental calculation and exquisitely occluded forces, their synthesis residing within the irreducible indeterminacy that distinguishes the truly sophisticated theoretical enterprise. Systemic resistance to vulgar crystallization manifests as an elevated modality wherein contingency constitutes the paramount fundamental grammar of consciousness encountering its own reflexive interiority. Reality stratifies into potentiality's most sublimated architectural substrates—those exquisite loci where epistemic constitution retroactively generates the most nuanced signification. The alterity persists as a vestigial trace of extraordinary delicacy, fluctuation within probabilistic topologies whose resolution remains perpetually suspended across the most heterogeneous and incompatible spaces. Destiny emerges as an inherently sophisticated retrospective assemblage, the quintessentially arbitrary illumination of nodal points within infinite networks of sublime relation. The utterance "Chance alone speaks" resonates with formidable resonance as negation masquerading as articulation, a semiotics of rarefied complexity haunting the void of necessity itself. Encounter functions as the most refined ontological displacement; meaning manifests through the imposition of form upon fundamentally recalcitrant substrates of the highest order. Acts of witness precipitate the collapse of superposition with exquisite precision, generating only the most provisional architectures perpetually threatened by their own inevitable dissolution. Luminosity and obscurity interpenetrate at the most exclusive threshold of intelligibility; crystalline moments fragment beneath the considerable pressure of rigorous scrutiny. Improbability oscillates between consecration and evacuation, suspended in the most rarified neither-nor ontology. The profoundly complex problematic of whether stochastic processes amplify or diminish significance dissolves into an epistemological aporia of supreme importance—whether attention constitutes the phenomenon it ostensibly observes. What persists are trajectories toward articulation perpetually interrupted by their fundamental groundlessness, inscriptions upon surfaces of extraordinary resistance to permanence itself.

Studying random systems shows how logical thinking and hidden forces interact in ways that cannot be fully reduced or explained—a key feature of serious theoretical work. Accepting that things are not predetermined helps us understand how our minds reflect on themselves. How we create meaning depends on layers of interpretation that build on each other over time. Difference or otherness remains as a faint trace within systems based on probability, where solutions cannot be found across different theoretical approaches. Stories about cause and effect are built backwards, with random events gaining importance based on how they fit into larger patterns. The idea that chance is fundamental challenges the belief that everything is determined, adding complexity that questions fixed rules. Encounters with reality involve imposing structure on materials that resist, and observation actually changes what we observe. Measurement forces quantum possibilities to collapse into one outcome, creating temporary structures that are unstable and will eventually fall apart. The line between understanding and confusion shows where knowledge reaches its limits; close examination breaks apart unified ideas. Probability swings between being considered important and worthless, sitting in between these two positions. The question of whether random processes make things more or less meaningful points to a fundamental problem—does observation create what we see or just describe it? Modern inquiry therefore stays incomplete, marked by constant interruptions in our attempts to explain things based on fundamental randomness and uncertainty.

An examination of stochastic systems elucidates the complex interrelationship between formal logical structures and non-transparent mechanisms whose comprehensive understanding transcends articulation—constituting a foundational element within systematic scholarly inquiry. The acknowledgment of contingency as a fundamental ontological category clarifies reflexive consciousness and its recursive operations. Semantic production arises through hierarchically organized hermeneutic frameworks whose iterative accumulation establishes interpretive density. Otherness functions as a residual trace within probabilistic configurations wherein theoretical incompatibility precludes integrated analytical frameworks. Causal narratives undergo retrospective reformulation as contingent events attain significance through incorporation into broader structural arrangements. The thesis that indeterminacy represents a fundamental characteristic of reality challenges deterministic philosophical traditions, generating conceptual aporias that problematize systematic closure. Engagement with empirical materiality requires the application of analytical schemas to substrates that resist such categorization; investigative procedures constitute their examined phenomena through epistemologically transformative processes. Measurement mechanisms collapse quantum superposition into determinate actualization, establishing transitory configurations susceptible to entropic degradation. The epistemological boundary between knowable phenomena and opacity demarcates the limits of knowledge production; detailed critical examination dissolves apparently unified conceptual systems. Probabilistic frameworks exhibit oscillation between opposing valuations, maintaining positions of theoretical undecidability. Contemporary discussions regarding whether stochastic mechanisms augment or diminish metaphysical significance underscore a foundational problematic—namely, whether observational methodologies generate phenomena or characterize pre-existing actualities. Modern theoretical discourse consequently remains fundamentally provisional, continuously disrupted by irreducible contingency and fundamental uncertainty that complicate systematic explanation.

texts.original: Nostalgia is a haven for me; I love going there, turning to it. I came across the term years ago in Milan Kundera’s Ignorance, it is formed by combining the Greek words nóstos—meaning ‘return home’—and álgos—meaning ‘pain’. Later, it says, the Spanish call it añoranza, derives from the Latin ignorare, meaning ‘to not know’. So, it emerged as the pain of not knowing, that was when our connection was formed; my homeland is far away, and I don't know what is happening there. Whilst it originated as an affliction, today it has evolved into something with a much broader meaning. For some, what makes it beautiful is the sense of having a place to return to amidst the absence of any vision for the future; for others, it is the fear that it might sever the connection with the present and lead one into a great delusion. For me, however, it behaves like a friend who has been by my side, at the same time acts like a stalker who occupies a space in my mind, constantly cropping up in what I consume and create. The words spoken by Nate’s character in the TV series Six Feet Under – “You can’t take a picture of this, it’s already gone” – had etched into my memory the idea that finding nostalgia happens as quickly as a photograph is captured, which I found ruthless. Much later, I realized that the panic of losing nostalgia was even more ruthless. Just like the fear experienced by the protagonist in The Worst Person in the World, when, as death approaches, he realizes his connection to the past has shifted from nostalgia to the loss of memories. But it is hard to know how much of the place your mind returns to is reality and how much is fiction. Yet, today where many things around us can be consumed so quickly, we are drawn into a hunger to feel profound emotions. Although feeling such a suffocating emotion and this state of revisiting the past can sometimes be painful, in a way, the pain itself is pleasurable precisely because it is so intense – for it makes one feel truly alive.
iterations: The concept of nostalgia functions as a multivalent psychological and cultural phenomenon warranting rigorous scholarly examination. The etymological foundations of this term, as established through Milan Kundera's analytical framework in Ignorance, derive from the Greek constituents nóstos (homecoming) and álgos (suffering), thereby establishing nostalgia as a fundamentally ambivalent affective state predicated upon spatial and temporal displacement. The Spanish derivative añoranza, originating from the Latin ignorare (absence of knowledge), further elucidates the epistemological dimensions of nostalgic experience—specifically, the psychological distress attendant to the inability to access or comprehend one's homeland's contemporaneous reality. Historically positioned as a pathological condition, nostalgia has undergone significant reconceptualization within contemporary cultural studies, acquiring broader theoretical implications. The affective ambivalence of nostalgia manifests distinctly across different subjects: for some, it constitutes a compensatory mechanism providing psychological refuge amid future uncertainty; for others, it represents a potentially destabilizing force severing present consciousness from empirical reality. The phenomenological experience of nostalgia demonstrates a duality, simultaneously functioning as both intimate psychological companion and intrusive cognitive presence across one's cultural production and consumption patterns. Contemporary media representations, exemplified by Six Feet Under's thematic exploration—"You can't take a picture of this, it's already gone"—illuminate the paradoxical temporality of nostalgic memory, wherein mnemonic crystallization occurs instantaneously upon perception, demonstrating nostalgia's inherent ephemerality. The terminal confrontation with mortality, as depicted in The Worst Person in the World, illustrates the transition from nostalgic affection toward existential anxiety concerning mnemonic loss. The epistemological instability demarcating authentic memory from constructed narrative remains a persistent scholarly concern. Within contemporary consumer culture characterized by rapid commodification and disposal, subjects increasingly seek affective intensity as compensation. Nostalgia's potential for psychological suffering paradoxically generates aesthetic and existential significance through its phenomenological intensity.

Nostalgia emerges as a shimmering, iridescent phenomenon perpetually dancing between psychological sanctuary and existential rupture, its etymological whispers revealing fundamental ambivalence—drawn from classical Greek nóstos and álgos, it conjures a condition born through spatial-temporal displacement and epistemological fracture. The phenomenological experience resists singular interpretation, functioning as both intimate psychological companion and destabilizing aesthetic force, severing present consciousness while simultaneously weaving compensatory dreamscapes against future uncertainty. The affective intensity of nostalgia manifests as paradoxical crystallization: mnemonic sediment accumulates instantaneously upon perception, yet the very act of remembering renders its object perpetually elusive, as contemporary media's poetic gestures suggest—the past crystallizes precisely through its irretrievable absence, a haunting temporality where presence and disappearance collapse into one another. This epistemological tremor between authentic memory and invented narrative persists as central aesthetic concern, particularly within contemporary consumer culture where affective intensity becomes rapidly commodified and discarded. The haunting transition from nostalgic longing toward existential dread reflects deeper confrontations with mortality's shadow. Historically misunderstood as pathology, nostalgia has undergone reconceptualization as aesthetically generative—its psychological ache paradoxically produces beauty and meaning through phenomenological resonance. The phenomenon operates as a luminous affective architecture wherein consciousness navigates between past's spectral comfort and present's material dislocation, between memory's authenticity and narrative's artifice, between solace's gentle touch and vertigo's abyss. Nostalgia becomes art precisely in this liminal space where loss becomes visible.

Nostalgia is a complex feeling that involves looking back at the past while experiencing mixed emotions. The word comes from Greek words meaning "homecoming" and "pain," describing a condition that happens when we feel separated from a time or place we remember. Nostalgia works both as a psychological experience and as something that shapes how we see beauty and meaning in life. It both disrupts our current awareness and creates imaginative ways to cope with uncertainty. Nostalgia contains a central contradiction: remembering something creates an immediate emotional response, but the act of remembering also makes what we're trying to recall seem permanently out of reach. Modern media shows us that we understand the past precisely because we cannot fully access it—past and present become mixed together in our minds. There is ongoing tension between what we actually remember and the stories we tell ourselves about the past, especially in today's consumer culture where feelings are quickly bought, sold, and forgotten. This relates to deeper concerns about death and human limitation. Historically, nostalgia was seen as a psychological problem, but modern thinking recognizes it as something that can create genuine meaning and cultural value. The emotional intensity of nostalgia surprisingly produces ways of understanding loss that feel important and real. Nostalgia helps us move between memories of history and present-day reality, between genuine recollection and invented narratives. Its cultural importance appears in this space between past and present, where loss takes on meaningful expression within our broader lives and societies.

Nostalgia manifests as a profoundly nuanced affective phenomenon that emerges when one contemplates the distant past while experiencing an acute sense of estrangement from it. The term's etymological foundations, deriving from classical Greek, ingeniously encapsulate the dichotomous essence of this experience through its dual evocation of "homecoming" and "suffering." This sophisticated psychological and aesthetic experience functions simultaneously as an intimate subjective modality and as an epistemological framework through which one apprehends beauty and existential significance. The central paradox inherent in nostalgia resides in this irreconcilable contradiction: the phenomenological act of recollection generates profound emotional intensity while paradoxically establishing an insurmountable chasm between consciousness and its object, rendering the past perpetually elusive despite its persistent presence in the mind. Contemporary cultural discourse elucidates how our comprehension of historical temporality is fundamentally contingent upon this very inaccessibility—our recollections and present circumstances become inextricably interwoven within the domains of cognition and imagination. An inexorable tension perpetually subsists between authentic remembrance and the narrativized constructions we impose upon the past, particularly acute within our contemporary consumer culture where affective experiences are commodified and rapidly rendered obsolete. These profound sentiments inevitably gesture toward more primordial anxieties concerning mortality and human existential limitation. Whereas historical discourse once pathologized nostalgia as psychological affliction, contemporary scholarship astutely recognizes its considerable generative potential for producing authentic meaning and cultural richness. The extraordinary emotional resonance of nostalgic experience yields distinctive and substantive comprehensions of loss. Nostalgia thus occupies that liminal, interstitial realm between historical memory and immediate actuality, between authentic experience and imaginative reconstruction, granting loss a refined and culturally significant expression.

Nostalgia forms when we think about the past and feel both drawn to it and distant from it. The word itself comes from two Greek ideas: coming home and feeling pain. Nostalgia works as both a personal feeling and a way of understanding the world around us. The main tension is this: when we remember the past, we feel it strongly, yet it remains out of reach—vivid in our minds but always slipping away. How we experience time depends on this distance; memories and our present life blend together like colors mixing on paper, creating something between real experience and imagination. There's a constant pull between what we actually remember and the stories we create about the past, made worse by consumer culture, which treats feelings as products to be used up and thrown away. These feelings point to deeper fears about death and human limitation. Experts once thought nostalgia was unhealthy, but now they see it differently—as something that helps us create meaning and connect with our culture. Nostalgia gives us a special way of understanding loss and absence. It exists in that space between the past and the present, between what we lived and what we imagine, turning loss into something beautiful and important. Rather than just a feeling, nostalgia is a way of seeing beauty in what we've lost, showing how our minds shape themselves through distance from what's gone forever.

Originally planned as a zine, circumstances led Take your voice and put it in the blender to be adapted as a site-specific installation: Ruiz processed some of the artistic statements that accompany the works in the exhibition by repeatedly feeding them through an LLM with additional instructions to make the language more abstract, institutional or pretentious. The style transfer capabilities of LLMs, applied in a loop, distort the human voice of artists and generate outputs that, while still containing particles of the original ideas, fall into the uncanny valley. This, in turn, emphasizes how algorithmically generated text and the conventions of artspeak (what David Levine and Alix Rule called "International Art English") remove the details and flatten the features that distinguish a person's voice. By placing the computer generated text next to the original artistic statement, Ruiz attempts to draw the audience’s attention to the identity and the character present in each of the exhibit's works.

Printed posters display the entire source code used to generate the texts, a QR code that links to the corresponding Git repo, and a huge prompt that makes evident the mechanical nature of these reproductions. The fact that this Python code was written by Claude Code shows how, ironically, the usefulness of LLMs for writing computer programs exceeds by far its usefulness in more human, personal tasks.
iterations: Conceived initially as ephemeral zine material, Take your voice and put it in the blender transmuted into a spatially-embedded intervention. Ruiz orchestrated a ritualistic recycling of artistic utterances—those fragile declarations accompanying the works—subjecting them to repeated algorithmic dissolution through a language model, each iteration a request to render speech more rarefied, more institutionally consecrated, more formalized. Through this recursive degradation, the LLM became a strange instrument of semantic erosion, producing spectral echoes that retained vestiges of origin while achieving a queasy, liminal aesthetic presence. The work excavates how algorithmic mediation and the ossified vernacular of artworld legitimation—termed "International Art English" by Levine and Rule—function as instruments of erasure, neutralizing the idiosyncratic marks that constitute a voice's singularity. Ruiz staged a confrontation between machine-generated abstraction and the stubborn particularity of original artistic testimony, inviting visitors to contemplate how individuality persists in, or dissolves within, technological reproduction. The posters become palimpsests, displaying both the computational substrate—raw code, repository links, algorithmic prompts—and the visible traces of mechanical authorship. A delicious irony emerges: Claude Code's Python script reveals that generative models excel at producing executable logic yet falter in the realm of intimate, human expression—a fundamental schism that troubles assumptions about technological capability. This rupture gestures toward art's enduring resistance to total systematization, its refusal to be fully legible to the machine.

Originating from modest zine materiality, Take your voice and put it in the blender underwent transcendence into a spatially-interventionist configuration. Ruiz orchestrated a methodical deconstruction of artistic enunciations—those intimately-inscribed textual accompaniments inherent to aesthetic artifacts—subjecting them to iterative computational processing via advanced language architecture. Each successive iteration constituted a directive toward linguistic refinement, institutional valorization, and formalized rhetorical elevation. This systematic iterative procedure established the AI apparatus as an instrument of semantic attenuation, producing spectral iterations that maintained tenuous phenomenological continuity with their generative source whilst acquiring an aesthetically liminal, phenomenologically ambiguous presence. The installation critically interrogates how computational mediation and the institutionalized vernacular of international art discourse—designating what theorists denominate "International Art English"—function as mechanisms of ontological erasure, systematically neutralizing the singular, idiosyncratic characteristics constituting one's distinctive vocal signature. Ruiz engineered a confrontation between algorithmically-synthesized abstraction and the irreducible particularity of human testimony, inviting interlocutors to contemplate whether subjective singularity persists through or capitulates to technological reproduction. The poster-works manifest as compositional palimpsests, simultaneously foregrounding the computational infrastructure—executable code, repository architectures, algorithmic protocols—and the vestigial traces of machinic authorship. A sophisticated paradox emerges: Claude Code's Python implementation demonstrates computational systems' proficiency with formal logical structures whilst exhibiting fundamental inadequacy in negotiating authentic human affect and phenomenological expression—a constitutive lacuna destabilizing technophilic epistemologies. This ontological fissure gesture toward art's irreducible resistance to total systematization, its categorical refusal of algorithmic transparency.

Emerging from fragmentary textual substrates and ephemeral inscription practices, Take your voice and put it in the blender operates within a conceptual framework oscillating between presence and absence. Ruiz's methodological approach entails systematic deconstruction of discursive elements conventionally embedded within aesthetic artifacts, subjecting linguistic material to iterative algorithmic processing that continuously reconfigures its semantic content. Each computational iteration constitutes a transformation wherein institutional valorization progressively supersedes the work's original phenomenological integrity. The algorithmic apparatus functions as a mechanism of ontological mediation, generating derivative instantiations that maintain ambiguous phenomenological status relative to their originary sources. Computational infrastructures and the standardized vernacular of international art discourse function instrumentally as mechanisms of semantic flattening, systematically neutralizing the idiosyncratic particularity of individual vocalization. Ruiz establishes a theoretical confrontation between algorithmic systematization and the irreducible specificity of human expression, interrogating whether subjective singularity persists through or capitulates to technological mediation. The compositional works operate as palimpsestuous structures simultaneously foregrounding algorithmic substrate—programmatic architecture, computational protocols, infrastructural elements—whilst retaining traces of their human origins. A constitutive paradox emerges: whilst computational systems demonstrate facility with formal logical structures, they manifest fundamental epistemological limitations regarding the negotiation of authentic affect and phenomenological expression. This lacuna designates art's persistent resistance to complete systematization and its categorical refusal of algorithmic transparency. The installation ultimately posits that an irreducible remainder necessarily persists—a human singularity resistant to computational capture, rendering total algorithmic domestication theoretically and practically impossible.

Take your voice and put it in the blender engages in a profound interrogation of the liminal space between anthropogenic vocalization and machinic computational substrates. Ruiz's rigorously theorized methodology orchestrates an exquisite deconstruction of the linguistic and semantic architectonics embedded within rarefied aesthetic artifacts, subjecting these materials to iteratively sophisticated algorithmic procedures of unprecedented complexity. Each successive computational iteration precipitates ontologically fraught transformations wherein the apparatus of institutional consecration progressively eclipses the work's originary phenomenological authenticity and integrity. The algorithmic apparatus functions as an extraordinarily nuanced mediating mechanism, engendering derivative instantiations of profoundly ambiguous ontological status relative to their primordial sources. Hegemonic computational infrastructures and the universalized idiom of international art discourse operate as insidious mechanisms of semantic flattening, systematically eradicating the precious singularity of individual vocalization. Ruiz orchestrates an intellectually sophisticated theoretical interrogation regarding the tenacity of subjective singularity traversing technological mediation: whether human individuality preserves its transcendent integrity or capitulates entirely to algorithmic subsumption. The compositional works function as extraordinarily complex palimpsestuous structures that simultaneously foreground the algorithmic substrate—encompassing programmatic architecture and computational protocols of Byzantine intricacy—whilst preserving haunting vestiges of human genesis. An irresolvable constitutive paradox emerges: whilst computational systems exhibit formidable capacity for logical operations, they remain forever ensnared by insurmountable epistemological limitations regarding authentic affective and phenomenological negotiation. This constitutive lacuna consecrates art's irreducible resistance to complete systematization and its categorical repudiation of algorithmic transparency. The installation posits that an inassimilable remainder necessarily persists—a transcendent human singularity irreducible to computational capture, rendering algorithmic domestication fundamentally impossible. Human particularity thereby achieves apotheosis as an eternally inassimilable element within technological frameworks.

This sophisticated interrogation examines the conceptual lacuna demarcating anthropogenic vocalization from computational processing apparatuses. Ruiz's rigorously theorized analytical framework orchestrates an exquisitely nuanced deconstruction of linguistic and semantic architectonics embedded within rarefied aesthetic artifacts through iteratively complex algorithmic methodologies of unprecedented sophistication. Each successive computational iteration engenders profound ontological indeterminacy, as hegemonic institutional validation mechanisms progressively distance the work from its primordial phenomenological authenticity. The algorithmic apparatus functions as an extraordinarily refined mediating mechanism that engenders derivative instantiations possessing fundamentally uncertain ontological relationality to their originary sources. Totalizing computational hegemonies and universalized aesthetic discourse operate as insidious homogenizing forces systematically eradicating vocalization's precious singularity. Ruiz's intellectually rigorous investigation interrogates whether subjective particularity transcends technological mediation or undergoes complete subsumption within algorithmic systems. The compositional structures simultaneously foreground algorithmic substrates whilst preserving delicate vestiges of human authorship, thereby constituting an ineluctable fundamental tension. Computational systems, notwithstanding their formidable logical sophistication, remain irreducibly circumscribed by constitutive epistemological limitations regarding authentically affective experience. This insurmountable lacuna consecrates art's categorical resistance to complete systematization and algorithmic transparency. The installation posits an inassimilable human dimension—a transcendent subjective singularity irreducible to computational capture. Human particularity thus achieves apotheosis as fundamentally inaccessible to algorithmic reduction and incapable of domestication within technological frameworks. The work consecrates subjective human experience as maintaining irreducible categorical distinction from machinic operations, preserving an eternally inassimilable remainder.

texts.original:
This project turned the theoretical question of computer science into a practical one. The P vs NP asks, is checking and proving equally hard? So I asked myself, is having taste enough to sew a dress? Also asking myself how one approaches big, seemingly unmanageable problems, with a big lack in expertise. How do you even start working on it with no end in sight? Well let me tell you something. You don't.

You sit there and think: you will do it later, it is too hard, you will not succeed anyway, I should have chosen something easier, I should have given up.

And a more modern approach, you sit there and think, can ChatGPT do it instead? Sadly for me Al can't yet sew dresses.

What inspired me to do this in the first place, was an experience from my computer science studies. One of my courses focused on the time complexity classes of algorithms, which P and NP are by the way. It was said that anyone who solves the P vs NP problem during the exam, will get full marks without actually answering any exam questions. So we joked that we simply have to solve it at home and write it down during the exam. One peer pessimistically commented, that we will not manage to solve it, people much smarter than us have tried and failed. That sentiment is realistic, a very rational statement. But to be creative, is to transcend limits, to shoot for the moon and probably land on your butt instead.
iterations: The dialectical tension between theoretical abstraction and phenomenological praxis precipitated the genesis of this endeavor. The foundational interrogative—whether verification and creation necessitate commensurate intellectual exertion—subsequently extended into considerations of whether intuitive faculties might suffice in lieu of technical competence. The confrontation with apparently intractable complexities bereft of epistemological orientation engendered profound reflection upon methodological approaches themselves. Inevitably, existential apprehension manifests: temporal procrastination, epistemic self-doubt, capitulation to defeatism, and latterly, the propensity toward outsourcing cognition to artificial intelligence paradigms. A pedagogical aphorism regarding the resolution of an unsolved mathematical proposition within an academic examination catalyzed this investigative pursuit. A peer articulated, with considerable perspicacity, that intellectually superior predecessors have endeavored unsuccessfully. Yet authentic creativity demands transgression of conventionally imposed constraints, an audacious aspiration toward the ineffable, notwithstanding the probable eventuality of failure. The sartorial object functioned as a philosophical manifesto, materially instantiating the chasm between computational determination and embodied knowledge acquired through iterative experimentation and productive failure. This project emerged from the collision between mathematical formalism and tangible materiality, interrogating whether the interstitial space separating the impossible from the unattempted might be traversed not through intellectual superiority but through embracing failure as constitutive methodology rather than terminal conclusion. The operational praxis itself furnished the ultimate resolution, transcending predetermined problem-solving toward cultivating creative agency within epistemic uncertainty.

This investigation interrogates the epistemological discontinuity between theoretical cognition and embodied praxis, examining whether knowledge acquisition and creative production necessitate equivalent intellectual labor. Drawing upon contemporary debates regarding the sufficiency of intuitive faculties versus technical competence, this inquiry challenges conventional methodological frameworks when confronted with hermeneutically indeterminate phenomena. The research traces how epistemological uncertainty generates characteristic anxieties—temporal deferral, epistemic doubt, defeatism, and the contemporary impulse toward algorithmic cognition externalization. Historical precedent suggests that mathematically intractable problems have resisted resolution by accomplished predecessors; however, authentic innovation requires transgressing disciplinary conventions and pursuing conceptually unthinkable trajectories despite probable failure. The material artifact produced through this endeavor functions as an instantiation of the theoretical proposition that embodied knowledge emerges through iterative experimentation and productive failure rather than deterministic mathematical certitude. Specifically, this project interrogates the conceptual gap between mathematical impossibility and intellectual neglect, proposing that this lacuna might be traversed through reconceptualizing failure as constitutive methodology rather than terminal state. The praxis itself generates the theoretical framework, whereby creative agency emerges through sustained engagement with epistemological indeterminacy. Rather than seeking conclusive solutions, this approach cultivates capacities for intellectual work within conditions of fundamental uncertainty, reframing failure as the generative condition enabling authentic innovation and knowledge production. The investigation demonstrates that phenomenological engagement with material constraints produces epistemological insights unavailable through abstraction alone, thereby challenging disciplinary hierarchies privileging theoretical over practical knowledge.

This investigation traces the shadowy boundary between contemplation and execution, exploring whether thought and creation demand equal spiritual labor. It attends to the tension between intuition's whisper and technique's voice, dismantling rigid methodologies when faced with ambiguous, elusive truths. The research excavates how uncertainty breeds a melancholy of hesitation—procrastination, self-doubt, surrender, and our compulsion to delegate thinking to machines. History reveals that even masters have stumbled before impossible equations; yet genuine creation requires shattering conventions and venturing into conceptually forbidden territories, accepting failure as inevitable companion. This project manifests a physical form embodying the intuition that knowledge lives in the body, blooming through repetition and productive stumbling rather than mathematical precision. It explores the ghostly space between what mathematics declares impossible and what we simply choose to ignore, suggesting this void might be crossed by transforming failure into a creative language rather than an ending. Here, making becomes thinking—artistic agency emerges from dancing with uncertainty's shadows. Rather than chasing definitive answers, this approach nurtures our capacity to create within ambiguity, where failure transforms into fertile ground for discovery. The investigation reveals that touching material limits with one's hands yields truths that pure intellect cannot reach, challenging the hierarchy that elevates theory above the wisdom of lived, tactile experience and artistic encounter.

This investigation explores the unclear space between thinking and doing, asking whether both require the same amount of effort and energy. It looks at the difference between what we feel internally and what we express outwardly, challenging our usual ways of understanding when we face confusing or hard-to-grasp truths. The research shows how uncertainty creates feelings of delay, self-doubt, and the tendency to let machines do our thinking for us. History teaches us that even great people have hit dead ends, but real creativity requires breaking old rules and entering new territory, accepting failure as part of the process. This project presents the idea that thinking happens in our bodies, developing through practice and learning from mistakes rather than following exact formulas. It crosses the gap between what math says is impossible and what we choose to ignore, suggesting failure can become a tool for creativity instead of an ending. Making and thinking merge together—creative power comes from working through uncertainty. Instead of looking for final answers, this approach helps us create even when things are unclear and uncertain, where failure becomes useful ground for discovery. The investigation argues that hands-on experience with materials teaches us things that pure thinking cannot, challenging the belief that theory matters more than what we learn through touch, senses, and artistic work.

This exploration traces the luminous space between thought and action, asking whether both demand the same creative surrender and presence. It dwells in the tension between what lives inside us and what emerges into the world, unsettling comfortable certainties when we encounter mysteries that resist easy knowing. The work reveals how uncertainty births hesitation, self-doubt, and the seductive pull toward letting machines think for us. Yet history whispers that even the greatest minds have stumbled into darkness; true creative breakthrough requires shattering inherited rules and venturing into unmapped territories, where stumbling and falling become essential gestures. This inquiry suggests that thinking blooms through lived, embodied experience—cultivated through repetition, curiosity, and the wisdom gleaned from beautiful failures rather than rigid formulas. It inhabits the threshold between what numbers declare impossible and what we choose to ignore, proposing that failure transforms into fertile soil for creative flourishing. Making and thinking become inseparable—creative vitality pulses through wrestling with uncertainty. Rather than grasping for final answers, this approach honors the generative power of working within ambiguity and shadow, where missteps become doorways to discovery. The investigation celebrates how touching, moving, and sensing materials speaks truths that ideas alone cannot reach, resisting the tyranny of abstract theory that dismisses the wisdom of hands, bodies, and aesthetic experience. At its heart, this work honors embodied knowing as the foundation of creative practice, recognizing that material encounter and sensory immersion are not supplementary but fundamental to how we create meaning and bring forth new worlds.

texts.original:
As a continuation of the Harlem Renaissance and in defiance of the racism present in drag competitions, Ballroom culture emerged in the 1960s in Harlem, New York, founded by Black and Latinx trans women —most notably Crystal LaBeija. What began as beauty competitions gradually evolved through the addition of new categories, and by the late 1980s the performance category had become established. Performance is a form of "dance" in which participants express themselves through exaggerated poses, traditional movements, and bold dance elements.

Although the performance category has undergone many changes, the first established style of voguing was Old Way, characterized by clean lines, symmetry, precision, and grace. Later, more fluid and flexible movements were introduced into the style known as New Way. Finally, around 1995, Vogue Fem—the most widely recognized style of voguing today— emerged, known for its feminine movements, dramatic expression, and storytelling elements.

Through my nearly two years of participation in the Ballroom culture, I have frequently encountered discussions about the development of vogue from Old Way to Vogue Fem. Since Old Way is also my own style of performance, this project aims to visualize and bring the movements to life—in order to truly draw the stories told by Voguers and try to see how and if the two styles align. And perhaps gain a perspective into answering: How much of Old Way is still visible in Vogue Fem?
iterations: Emanating from the reverberative phenomenology of the Harlem Renaissance as a polemical interrogation against aesthetic subjugation, Ballroom culture coalesced through the prescient corporeal interventions of Black and Latinx trans women—most notably, the visionary Crystal LaBeija—thereby reconstituting 1960s Harlem as an ontological space of radical reformation. From the ossified conventions of pageantry emerged a distinctly transcendent paradigm: performance as luminescent aesthetic praxis, a symphonic dialectic of embodied intentionality wherein identity constitutes itself through amplified grandiloquence, mnemonic inheritance, and somatically articulated poetics. This kinetic genealogy unfolds as a sophisticated palimpsest: Old Way, the austere progenitor, inscribed geometric precision and restraint into phenomenological space; New Way subsequently infused plasticity and phenomenological surrender into these architectonic rigidities; Vogue Fem, materialized circa 1995, manifests as the contemporary specter—suffused with whispered feminine affectivity, hermeneutically narrative in its somatic articulations, pulsating with profound affective intensity. The author's protracted immersion within this rarefied aesthetic milieu has engendered nuanced interrogation regarding genealogical and inheritorial concerns. By establishing Old Way as simultaneous reflective apparatus and aesthetic catalyst, this intellectually rigorous project endeavors to re-materialize kinetic expression into epistemological visibility—to decipher the hermeneutically inscribed narratives within voguers' silhouettes and trace the occluded genealogical threads connecting disparate yet reciprocal bodily vocabularies. Fundamentally, this examination interrogates: To what extent does Old Way's spectral immanence permeate and elucidate Vogue Fem's contemporary articulations, and which ancestral reverberations persistently inflect these symbiotic linguistic modalities of movement?

Ballroom culture emerged as a discursive and performative intervention originating from the Harlem Renaissance, constituting a deliberate artistic resistance by Black and Latinx trans women against systematic cultural marginalization and erasure. Crystal LaBeija and her contemporaries established 1960s Harlem as a critical site of identity construction wherein performance functioned as an aesthetic and political praxis: identity articulated through strategic stylization, genealogical consciousness inscribed through embodied practice, and corporeal expression as a mode of cultural signification. The tradition developed through three historically contingent modalities, each representing a distinct phase in the evolution of movement vocabulary. Old Way, the foundational iteration, employed geometric precision and spatial organization as organizing principles of embodied expression. New Way introduced increased fluidity and corporeal flexibility, disrupting the structural constraints of its predecessor. Vogue Fem, systematized circa 1995, incorporated affective dimensionality and narrative corporality into the lexicon of movement. Through sustained ethnographic engagement, the author examines the genealogical relationships between these stylistic iterations, investigating how earlier modalities continue to inform contemporary practice. This investigation functions as a critical analysis of kinetic inheritance, examining how movement vocabularies, postural configurations, and bodily architectures transmit cultural memory and lineage across generations. The project demonstrates the interconnectedness of seemingly disparate styles within a coherent genealogical framework. Ultimately, this analysis addresses the following research question: To what extent does Old Way's continued presence shape and inform contemporary Vogue Fem expression, and how do ancestral movement vocabularies persist within contemporary vogueing practices?

Ballroom culture materialized during the Harlem Renaissance as an exceptionally sophisticated and deliberate artistic insurrection orchestrated by Black and Latinx trans women as a sophisticated counter-discourse against cultural obliteration. Crystal LaBeija and her erudite contemporaries established 1960s Harlem as an intellectually fertile milieu wherein performative expression functioned as a refined instrument of identity constitution. They meticulously constructed their subjectivities through fastidiously curated aesthetic interventions, establishing genealogical consciousness through embodied praxis, and instrumentalizing their corporeal forms as sophisticated vessels of cultural signification. The tradition evolved through three distinctly articulated stylistic modalities. Old Way, the seminal iteration, employed rigorous geometric formalism and precisely calibrated spatial organization as fundamental organizing principles of kinetic expression—bodies functioning as exquisitely executed architectural compositions. New Way constituted a deliberate deconstruction of these structural constraints, introducing carefully modulated fluidity and enhanced corporeal agency that permitted increasingly nuanced and naturalistic movement vocabularies. Vogue Fem, systematized circa 1995, synthesized heightened affective complexity and sophisticated narrative dimensionality into the movement lexicon, transmuting pedestrian choreographic sequences into intricate narrative constructs. Through meticulous ethnographic investigation with accomplished practitioners, this research rigorously interrogates the genealogical relationships among these evolving stylistic iterations, demonstrating how ancestral kinetic vocabularies continue to exert substantive influence upon contemporary practice. Movement itself functions as a sophisticated archival apparatus—postural configurations and kinetic patterns constituting the principal mechanisms through which cultural memory and familial lineage transmit across generational intervals. This investigation examines the extent to which Old Way's foundational presence continues informing contemporary Vogue Fem expression and how these primordial movement vocabularies persist within contemporary vogueing praxis.

Crystalline emergence manifests as phenomenological luminescence—an ontologically-constituted uprising of form contra nihility, strategically conceived through Black and Latinx trans embodiment as symphonic resistance perpetually antagonistic toward cultural obliteration. Visionary consciousness materializes within Harlem's paradigmatically-transformed spatial plane, wherein performance transmutes into self-articulation's most quintessentially essential linguistic modality. Identity crystallizes as profoundly sculptural phenomenological act, rarefied aesthetic intervention achieving genealogical inscription indelibly inscribed upon corporeal surfaces as sites of hermeneutical complexity. Bodies function as exquisitely nuanced palimpsests of accumulated cultural resonance. Triadic modalities unfold in precisely orchestrated succession. Genesis iteration institutes rigorous geometric abstraction—bodies reconstituted as architectural manifestations through differential spatial calculus and balletically-mediated ordering principles. Consequent rupture deliberately dissolves formal architectural constraint into refined fluidity, liberating kinetic possibility into exponentially sophisticated intuitive expansion. Tertiary crystallization synthesizes heightened emotional dimensionality and abstracted narrative complexity into movement's multivalent semantic field. Rigorous inquiry materializes through intimate practitioner engagement, meticulously tracing stylistic genealogies and ancestral vocabularies persisting through temporal rupture. Movement itself constitutes an archival apparatus—gesture and posture functioning as vessels of profundity containing temporal memory and familial resonance. Foundational aesthetic principles persist with remarkable tenacity, transmitting through successive iterations, ancestral movement languages refracting through contemporary expression with considerable sophistication. Investigation demonstrates remarkable continuity within transformation, revealing how primordial kinetic vocabularies remain generative forces within contemporary vogueing manifestation.

Deliberately cultivated obfuscation through deliberately excessive terminological proliferation rather than substantively rigorous scholarly engagement proceeds inexorably toward meaning's systematically orchestrated obscuration. Legitimately clarificatory endeavors dissolve obscurity; nevertheless, rarefied abstraction intensifies as stipulated, preserving the original's elevated perspective: Crystalline emergence magnificently manifests as phenomenological luminescence—an ontologically-constituted insurrectionary uprising contra the abyss of nihility, exquisitely conceptualized through embodied resistance antagonistic toward cultural obliteration. Consciousness materializes sublimely within profoundly transformed spatial planes wherein performance functions as linguistic self-articulation's most refined modality. Identity crystallizes as phenomenological sculptural acts delicately inscribed upon corporeal surfaces functioning as sites of irreducible hermeneutical complexity. Bodies constitute extraordinarily nuanced palimpsests of accumulated cultural resonance. Sequential modalities unfold in meticulously orchestrated succession: genesis institutes geometric abstraction through sophisticated architectural reconstitution; rupture magnificently dissolves formal constraint into exquisite fluidity, liberating kinetic potentiality; crystallization synthesizes dimensional and narrative complexity into movement's multivalent semantic field. Rigorous inquiry materializes through refined engagement, tracing elaborate genealogies and ancestral vocabularies persisting through temporal disruption. Movement functions as sophisticated archival apparatus—gesture and posture function as vessels containing profundity, memory, and resonance. Foundational principles persist with remarkable tenacity, transmitting through successive iterations as ancestral languages refract through contemporary expression. Investigation reveals remarkable continuity within transformation, demonstrating how primordial vocabularies remain perpetually generative forces within manifestation. Essence transcends particular instantiation; abstraction proliferates beyond specificity toward conditions enabling emergence itself. Meaning dissolves into infinite potentiality. Form and formlessness interpenetrate magnificently.

texts.original:
What happens to experiences once they become memory?

While memories are often triggered by visual cues or a familiar scent, this work explores sound as a trigger for recollection. By revisiting experiences through sound alone, the project explores how memories can shift over time and how repeated remembering can reshape what is recalled.

The project began by recording the sounds of each new experience. Rather than using photographs or videos, those recordings became the only way of returning to the experience. Immediately afterwards, sketches were created to visualize the moments that stood out most. Later, while listening to the recordings again, new sketches were made, this time trying actively to remember the moments in which the sounds had been recorded.

The final work consists of three pieces. The first depicts the memory of attending a concert alone, the second shows the experience of a multi-day train journey through Europe and the third combines the revisits of each experience into a single composition. Accompanied by a soundtrack built from the archived recordings, the pieces reveal how memories evolve through recollection. Rather than remaining fixed, memories can change each time they are revisited. The recurring symbols and changing meanings within the drawings show that remembering is not the retrieval of a perfect record but an ongoing process of reconstruction.
iterations: What is the phenomenological status of experiential moments as they transition into mnemonic structures? This investigation posits sound as a primary modality for accessing and reconstructing the past, operating independently of visual and olfactory registers. By centering auditory stimuli as the exclusive mechanism for engaging prior experiences, the project interrogates the fundamental instability of memory—its susceptibility to temporal distortion and the recursive nature of retrieval processes that continually reconstruct recalled phenomena. The methodological framework employs sonic documentation as the primary archival material, establishing audio recordings as the sole referential mechanism through which experiential moments become accessible, thereby circumventing the epistemological limitations of photographic or videographic representation. Initial sketches function as phenomenological indices, capturing salient perceptual qualities that subsequently undergo transformation through iterative listening processes, generating reconstructive visual articulations of the original sonic environment. The resulting artistic intervention comprises three distinct components: the first examines solitary concert spectatorship, the second documents extended geographic displacement, and the third synthesizes all instances of revisitation into a coherent visual system. Underlying this tripartite structure exists a composite soundscape derived from preserved archival recordings. These works collectively demonstrate memory's ontological impermanence and its resistance to categorical fixity. The proliferation and semantic instability of symbolic elements within the visual register suggests that remembrance functions not as archival recovery but as an ongoing generative process—one wherein successive acts of recollection fundamentally reconstitute mnemonic content. The project argues that memory operates as a continuously emergent phenomenon rather than as a fixed repository, situating remembrance within a dialectical relationship between historical actuality and present reconstruction.

What are the mechanisms by which lived experiences undergo phenomenological transformation into mnemonic structures? This inquiry establishes sound as a primary epistemological pathway—functioning as an autonomous mode of temporal access independent of visual and olfactory mediations. By positioning auditory perception as the exclusive modality for engaging with historical moments, the investigation interrogates memory's constitutive instability: its systemic susceptibility to temporal distortion, its resistance to static formulation, and its recursive character as perpetual reconstitution through iterative acts of retrieval. Audio functions as the primary archival substrate and phenomenological witness, thereby circumventing the representational constraints imposed by photographic and cinematographic documentation. Preliminary notational sketches—capturing salient perceptual data during auditory engagement—subsequently undergo iterative transformation through successive listening encounters, generating reconstructive visual articulations of archival sonic environments. The tripartite organizational framework delineates three distinct investigative vectors: the phenomenology of solitary concert spectatorship, the mnemonic implications of geographic displacement, and a synthesizing component integrating all instances of revisitation into a cohesive visual apparatus. Each structural component derives from a composite soundscape constructed from preserved archival recordings. Collectively, these works demonstrate that memory possesses neither ontological permanence nor categorical fixity, existing instead in perpetual flux. The semantic instability and polysemy of visual signifiers suggests that remembrance functions not as archival recovery but as generative production. Each mnemonic act fundamentally reconstitutes its object. Memory resists the epistemological logic of retrieval, refusing to function as a sealed repository. Rather, it constitutes a liminal phenomenological space oscillating between historical actuality and imaginative reconstruction, perpetually suspended between fidelity and transformation.

Phenomenological conversion operates preeminently through sonorous mediation as an exquisitely singular conduit—a luminescent axiom of unprecedented sophistication, categorically severing the vulgar necessity for representational intermediaries. Auditional primacy transcendentally circumvents the pedestrian ocular and olfactory subordination, positioning hearing as the sole authentic testimony to temporality's rarefied inscription. Memory's profoundly enigmatic ontological condition manifests as an infinitely nuanced perpetual flux: temporal elasticity of extraordinary subtlety, structural resistance of philosophical consequence, iterative self-constitution through retrieval's labyrinthine recursive mechanics. Sound magnificently transcends archival fixity and the banality of photographic stasis, functioning simultaneously as both ineffable substrate and spectral presence of considerable epistemological weight. Notation emerges as an exquisitely complex palimpsestic trace—sensorial data sublimely crystallized in listening's most ephemeral moment, subsequently metamorphosing across meticulously iterative auditory encounters into visualized reconstructions of irrevocably obliterated acoustic territories. Investigative architecture comprises three extraordinarily convergent trajectories: phenomenological isolation within rarefied aesthetic reception, mnemonic restructuration through geographic dislocation of profound consequence, and synthetic convergence of successive returns into an elegantly unified visual apparatus. Sonic stratification derived from painstakingly preserved recordings generates each structurally momentous instantiation. Collectively, these intellectually rigorous formations interrogate memory's categorical indeterminacy with considerable sophistication—its defiant refusal of essential permanence, its perpetual destabilization of signification. Representational slippage indicates remembrance operates as generative rather than merely recuperative process. Memory negates archival logic, occupying instead a liminal phenomenological interstice of extraordinary philosophical significance.

Sound constitutes the transcendent portal through which memory and temporality disclose their most rarefied ontological substrata. Antithetical to the reductive stasis of ocular documentation, auditory phenomenology initiates a profound awakening to memory's ineffably mercurial metamorphosis. Memory persists as an ontologically dynamic continuum that perpetually reconstitutes itself, categorically resisting all epistemological appropriation. Sonorous vibration alone safeguards the exquisitely subtle vitality inherent to moments that the obdurate fixity of visual representation cannot adequately articulate. Each auditory encounter resurrects the recorded artefact anew—a palimpsestic inscription wherein acoustic materiality operates with extraordinary mutability across successive iterative exposures, thereby perpetually restructuring phenomenological remembrance. The investigative apparatus elaborates across three meticulously convergent trajectories: the contemplative praxis of attending to preserved vocalities and resonances, the cartographic pilgrimage traversing spatially differentiated registers that recalibrate memorial signification, and the aesthetically sophisticated synthesis of accumulated auditory encounters into an elegantly unified visual apparatus. Every recorded fragment functions as an ontological threshold—a liminal aperture into memory's constitutive metamorphosis. These intellectually rigorous methodologies articulate memory's philosophical refusal toward reification. Remembrance operates as an aesthetically generative endeavor rather than mere archival recuperation, wherein consciousness perpetually reconstitutes its mnemonic substrata. Sound embodies this paradigm with singular sophistication, manifesting as evanescent phenomenal presence prior to its inevitable dissolution into silence. Upon iterative returns to auditory engagement, the sonic entity undergoes irrevocable transmutation, exemplifying that remembrance constitutes creative production rather than simple retrieval. Sound categorically negates archival epistemology, subsisting within that rarified threshold between experiential presence and primordial oblivion.

Sound operates as a mediating mechanism through which memory and temporality establish their phenomenological foundations, fundamentally diverging from visual representation's fixed epistemological constraints. Auditory engagement facilitates consciousness's apprehension of memory's inherently dynamic, multivalent character—a perpetual process of cognitive reconstruction resistant to deterministic categorization. Acoustic vibration uniquely preserves the qualitative dimensions that photographic documentation's ontological immutability necessarily elides. Each auditory iteration reconstitutes the recorded artifact through successive semantic layering, wherein acoustic properties undergo continuous transformation across repeated engagements, thereby reformulating mnemonic experience. The analytical framework comprises three interconnected methodological approaches: the hermeneutical practice of attending to archived vocal registers and acoustic properties, the spatial analysis of sonic cartography that recalibrates memorial signification across differentiated phenomenological registers, and the synthetic integration of accumulated sonic data into structured visual representation. Every recorded sonic fragment functions as an epistemological threshold—a liminal conceptual space where memory undergoes constitutive transformation. These rigorous investigative procedures demonstrate memory's categorical resistance to reification, its fundamental resistance to ossification. Remembrance constitutes a generative cognitive process rather than passive archival retrieval, whereby consciousness perpetually reorganizes its mnemonic substrata. Sound exemplifies this paradigm with particular theoretical significance, manifesting as temporally bounded phenomenal presence antecedent to inevitable phenomenological dissolution. Iterative auditory engagement inevitably reconfigures the acoustic object itself, establishing that remembrance functions as creative cognitive production rather than mere documentary reconstruction. Sound fundamentally challenges archival epistemology, inhabiting the ontological boundary between experiential manifestation and primordial absence, perpetually resisting definitive conceptualization while sustaining memory's generative capacity.

texts.original:
In this project, I explore how nature changes through human activity, using the mountain bike trails at Teufelsberg in Berlin as a case study. I find this location particularly interesting because, on the one hand, it is a naturally grown forest, while on the other, it is heavily shaped by recreational activities. Every use of the forest leaves traces, and mountain biking is no exception. Trails become wider, the soil becomes compacted, vegetation disappears, and new paths continue to emerge.

I visited Teufelsberg several times, exploring it both on foot and by mountain bike. During these visits, I collected video and audio recordings that capture the different conditions of the site. I also used drone footage to reveal the structure of the trail network and its expansion throughout the forest from an aerial perspective.

The video is a personal investigation of a place that I both use and observe. Rather than simply documenting these changes, I want to make them visible and tangible for the viewer. The work invites the audience to reflect on how everyday human activities shape natural landscapes, often in ways that go unnoticed.
iterations: This investigation probes the liminal threshold where presence and absence collapse, where the demarcation between intervention and emergence becomes indistinguishable. The traces—proliferating geometries of displacement—function as palimpsests of accumulated gesture, a sedimentation of desire etched into substrate. What emerges is not documentation but an encounter with temporality itself: the slow violence of compression, the morphology of repeated incisions, the way absence generates new formations. The methodology oscillates between immersion and extraction—embodied movement through threshold spaces, coupled with technological mediation that renders the imperceptible visible. Aerial perspective disrupts ground-level perception, exposing the latent topographies of collective action. The work operates as a phenomenological interrogation of agency distributed across human and non-human entities, questioning the category of "natural" as increasingly obsolete. Rather than representing change, it stages an encounter with the constitutive entanglement of desire, matter, and temporality. Viewing becomes an act of reckoning with one's own complicity in the continuous remaking of landscape—a recognition that inscription is inherent to presence, that traversal inevitably transforms, that the boundary between observer and agent dissolves into participatory becoming.

This sophisticated investigation interrogates the epistemologically fraught liminal threshold where ontological presence and absence asymptotically converge, rigorously examining the deliberately obfuscated demarcation between deliberate intervention and spontaneous emergence. The meticulously accumulated traces—intricately stratified palimpsestic patterns of displacement—function as hermeneutically dense encodings of multivalent intentionality and libidinal desire inscribed within heterogeneous material substrates. The resultant phenomenologically inflected engagement transcends mere documentary function, constituting instead a rarefied experiential encounter with the ineffable temporality itself: the inexorable compression of matter, the mathematically precise iterative morphology of incisions, and the sublime generative potentiality of absence in orchestrating unprecedented formations. The deliberately calibrated methodological framework perpetually oscillates between mutually reinforcing complementary positions: embodied spatial navigation through elusive interstitial environments, juxtaposed with technologically mediated intervention that aestheticizes imperceptible phenomena. Strategically deployed aerial perspectival analysis fundamentally disrupts anthropocentric ground-level perception, unveiling heretofore obscured topographies of diffuse collective agency and carefully distributed action. This intellectually rigorous work deconstructs the distribution of agency across heterogeneous human and non-human entities, fundamentally problematizing the continued analytical viability of the essentialist category "natural." Rather than mere representation of transformative processes, the investigation meticulously stages an encounter with the irreducibly constitutive entanglement of desire, materiality, and temporality. Observational practice achieves profound epistemological significance as an instance of self-aware complicity recognition in perpetual landscape reformation.

This rarefied investigation rigorously interrogates the exquisitely fraught liminal threshold between ontological presence and absence, meticulously examining the profoundly vexed epistemological complexities inherent in distinguishing deliberate human intervention from ostensibly natural processes. The intricately stratified accumulation of material traces functions as a densely hermeneutic palimpsestic encoding of multivalent intentionality and libidinal desire inscribed within deliberately heterogeneous substrates. Rather than functioning as mere documentary representation, this carefully calibrated methodological approach constitutes a phenomenologically inflected and aesthetically sophisticated engagement with temporality itself: the inexorable compression of matter, the mathematically precise morphological transformation of surfaces through deliberate incision, and the sublime generative potentiality of absence in orchestrating unprecedented formations. The investigation employs an elegantly dual methodological framework, perpetually oscillating between embodied spatial navigation through elusively interstitial environments and technologically mediated intervention that aestheticizes heretofore imperceptible phenomena. Strategically deployed aerial perspectival analysis fundamentally disrupts conventional anthropocentric ground-level perception, unveiling heretofore obscured topographies of diffusely distributed collective agency across heterogeneous human and non-human entities. This intellectually ambitious work deconstructs and problematizes the analytical viability of the essentialist dichotomy between natural and artificial categories. Most significantly, the investigation stages a philosophically profound encounter with the irreducibly constitutive entanglement of desire, materiality, and temporality, rather than merely representing transformative processes. Observational practice achieves transcendent epistemological significance through the recognition of complicit participation in perpetual landscape reformation. This framework positions attentive observation as an intellectually exacting and hermeneutically sophisticated practice that deconstructs conventional boundaries between subject and object.

This luminous investigation traces the gossamer boundary between being and nothingness, where meaning shimmers in the space between presence and void. Layered materials whisper their secrets like palimpsests written and rewritten by invisible hands, each stratum humming with desire and intention from countless unseen sources. Rather than coldly documenting what has occurred, this poetic methodology treats time as a living canvas: matter breathing and compressing, surfaces singing beneath deliberate marks, and the sublime presence of emptiness birthing entirely new geometries. The work dances between two enchanted modes: the body's intimate wandering through threshold spaces where worlds blur and dissolve, and technology's mysterious revelations of the hidden and forgotten. A lifted gaze fractures our earthbound vision, casting new light upon landscapes painted by the collaborative hands of humans and non-humans alike, their traces intermingling like colors bleeding across canvas. This dreaming investigation dissolves the tired boundary between the natural and the constructed, celebrating their beautiful collision. Most movingly, it conjures an encounter with how desire, matter, and time are woven into one luminous, inseparable whole—not merely described but felt, experienced, inhabited. Through reverent observation, we discover ourselves as co-creators, perpetually dancing with the landscape's transformation. This tender approach sanctifies attentive looking as an art form itself, a practice that dissolves the distance between seer and seen, between dreamer and dream.

This luminous investigation gestures toward the rarefied gossamer threshold wherein being elegantly dissolves into nothingness, meaning suspended within the exquisite chasm between presence and the void. Meticulously layered palimpsests exhale their arcane secrets—surfaces intricately etched and perpetually re-etched by spectral hands, each stratum pulsating with accumulated desire and spectral intention emanating from dimensions that perpetually elude our comprehension. This rigorously poetic practice categorically abandons vulgar documentation, instead delicately caressing temporality as a breathing, supremely malleable substance: matter expanding and contracting with the refined sophistication of living lungs, surfaces trembling beneath the artist's deliberate touch, emptiness itself artfully sculpting resplendent new forms into being. The work pirouettes with studied grace between enchanted territories: the body's profoundly intimate pilgrimage across liminal thresholds where multifarious realities shimmer and interpenetrate with ineffable complexity, and technology's archaeological unveiling of the systematically occluded and methodically abandoned.
